The underlying requests library does the same thing:

https://github.com/requests/requests/blob/master/requests/sessions.py#L397-L398

Supplying an empty `dict` should result in the same behavior. Take for example the following:

```
>>> def foo(bar='baz'):
...     print(bar)
...
>>> foo(**{})
baz
>>>
```

That said, this _could_ change so hid this initialization behind a guard so that it only runs _if_ `adapter_kwargs` was supplied.

First, no it is not equivalent, but I get the idea. Actually, the code does not terminate, as `index` is not updated. But the following code works:

```python
while parent[index] != parent[parent[index]]:
    parent[index] = parent[parent[index]]
return parent[index]
```

This code leads to the same max-tree, but it is still different in that there is no path compression: in the recursive formulation, all entries of parent along the path between `index` and `root` will point to `root` at the end. In the code above, the `parent` array is only updated at `index`. Algorithmically, the code you proposed should therefore be less performant. 

_But_: you are completely right that there is something to be gained by having a non-recursive function that can be inlined. I tested this, and on `skimage.data.camera()` for instance I gain 20 ms (7 %) with your version. So it seems that inlining more than compensates what is lost by path compression. However, I have no clear view on what happens on bigger images.

```py
class Quantile(VariableTemplate):
    q = None
    variable = None
    weight_variable = None
    filter_variable = None
    value_type = int

    def make_formula(self):
        assert self.q is not None
        assert self.variable is not None

        def formula(entity, period):
            value = entity(self.variable, period)
            if self.weight_variable is not None:
                weight = entity(self.weight_variable, period)
            weight = entity.filled_array(1)
            if self.filter_variable is not None:
                filter_value = entity(self.filter_variable, period)
                weight = filter_value * weight

            labels = arange(1, self.q + 1)
            quantile, _ = mark_weighted_percentiles(
                value,
                labels,
                weight,
                method = 2,  # * filter,
                return_quantiles = True,
                )
            if self.filter_variable is not None:
                quantile = where(weight > 0, quantile, -1)
            return quantile

        return formula

class decile_salaire_from_quantile(Quantile, Variable):

    label = u"Décile de salaire nouveau calcul"
    definition_period = YEAR
    q = 10
    variable = 'salaire'
```

While I'm not opposed to the idea of better granularity when it comes to managing operation timeouts, I don't think that will work for this case. For the `socket` library, connection timeouts and socket timeouts are the same thing. Only one timeout value can be set, via `socket.settimeout(...)`, and it must be set before a connection is established to another socket. I do not believe that it can be reset once a connection is established; that would create unstable behavior. This timeout value applies to all subsequent socket operations, including `socket.connect`, `socket.sendall`, `socket.recv`, etc. (i.e., all `socket` operations used by the client).

Request messages in the KMIP 1.1 specification do not specify timeout limits for operations either. However, KMIP requests can specify the maximum response size (in bytes) that should be returned by the server (for large operation responses). It is also possible to execute an operation on a KMIP appliance as an asynchronous operation. In this case, the appliance should return an asynchronous correlation value (i.e., an operation token) that can be used to poll the appliance for the status of the operation. PyKMIP does not currently support either of these features.

Three off-topic comments:
  * IMO, Java serialization is worse than reflection. Reflection is very explicit about what it does, and can be controlled at least to some degree by a `SecurityManager`. Serialization _creates objects without invoking constructors_, which violates a core invariant…so forces you to implement these special `readObject` methods…which force you to initialize fields outside of constructors…so you can't mark your fields `final` even when they actually should be, resulting in a (private) API change…<sub>unless you use reflection…</sub>
  * IMO, Python's `f.func_closure` is far worse than `f.func_defaults`.
  * The good news about `f.func_closure` is that I tried very hard to find a way to mutate those values, and have a sketch of a proof that—at least in CPython—it is not possible without resorting to extension modules.

This is going to crash if the pipe buffer fills up – `os.write` will raise `BlockingIOError` (EWOULDBLOCK), and we don't have anything to catch it. In `SocketStream.send_all`, it has a slightly easier job, because it doesn't call the raw OS `send` function directly – it calls the `trio.socket` `send` method, and that takes care or retrying and waiting. See `_nonblocking_helper` and `_try_sync` in `trio.socket` for the gory details... not that they're perfect, they're probably a bit over-elaborate themselves.
